conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I'm inquiring about my two children - Alex who is 17, and Blake who is 3.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex (17) and Blake (3).

Let me start with some questions about your situation:

Do you live in the UK, or are you otherwise responsible for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. Both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. 

Next, I need to ask: Are either Alex or Blake currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training? This is particularly relevant for Alex as they're 17.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. Blake is only 3, so they're not in school yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand a bit more about Alex's situation.

You mentioned Alex is 17 and not currently in education or training. Has Alex left education recently, and if so, when did they leave? Also, has Alex registered with a career service or with HM Revenue and Customs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex left school a few months ago, but no, they haven't registered with any careers service or anything like that yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to ensure I have all the information needed.

First, are you the person responsible for both Alex and Blake? In other words, are you their parent or the main person who looks after them and is responsible for their care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm their parent and I'm responsible for both of them. They both live with me and I look after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions:

Is anyone else also claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, or living with them and potentially eligible to claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them both.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for all that information. Just one final question:

Are either Alex or Blake in hospital or in care (for example, being looked after by a local council)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital or in care. They're both at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

**For Blake (age 3): You ARE eligible ✓**
Blake is under 16, lives with you, you're responsible for them, and all other conditions are met. You can claim Child Benefit for Blake.

**For Alex (age 17): You are NOT eligible ✗**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex. For children aged 16 or 17, Child Benefit can only be claimed if they are:
- In approved full-time non-advanced education or training, OR
- Have recently left education/training and registered with a careers service or HMRC (for up to 20 weeks)

Since Alex left school a few months ago but hasn't registered with a careers service or HMRC, they don't meet the eligibility criteria.

**What you could consider:**
If Alex registers with a careers service or HMRC now, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for a limited period (up to 20 weeks from when they left education). Alternatively, if Alex starts approved education or training, you could become eligible to claim for them again.

You should proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Blake. If you have any questions about Alex's situation or need help understanding the registration process, please let me know!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and not in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can only claim Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or 17 if they are in approved education or training, or if they have left education/training and are registered with a career service or with HMRC (and only for a limited period of up to 20 weeks). Since Alex left school a few months ago and has not registered with a careers service or HMRC, they do not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16. The claimant is Blake's parent, lives in the UK, is responsible for Blake who lives with them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake is not in hospital or care. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a parent living in the UK with two children: Alex (17) and Blake (3). They are responsible for both children who live with them. Blake, being 3 years old, meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit. However, Alex at 17 is not eligible because they are not in approved education or training and have not registered with a careers service or HMRC after leaving school. Child Benefit for 16-17 year olds requires them to be in qualifying education/training or registered appropriately after leaving.
